This allows you to perform calculations, transformations, or logical operations on existing fields without needing to create new fields in the database. This is very useful for reporting purposes.

Key Benefits of Local Fields:

  • Flexibility: Create custom calculations and derive new information without modifying the underlying data structure.
  • Performance: Calculations are performed during query execution, avoiding the need to store redundant data.
  • Simplicity: Define complex logic directly within the query, making it easier to manage and maintain.

Example: Calculating Delivery Variance (Your Scenario)

Let's use your example of the BACKLOGVENTES InfoSet to illustrate how to define a local field for delivery variance:

Scenario: You want to see the difference between the referenced quantity (DELIVERY) and the confirmed quantity (SALES ORDER). This difference represents the delivery variance.

Steps to Define a Local Field:

  1. Open SAP Query (SQ01/SQ02): Navigate to SAP Query using transaction codes SQ01 (Queries) or SQ02 (InfoSets).

  2. Edit/Create a Query: Open the relevant query based on the BACKLOGVENTES InfoSet.

  3. Go to the "Fields" tab: This is where you select the fields to be included in your report.

  4. Define a Local Field:

    • Click on the "Local Fields" button (usually represented by an icon of a calculator or a field with a plus sign).

    • Enter a name for your local field (e.g., DELIVERY_VARIANCE).

    • Enter a description (e.g., "Difference between Referenced and Confirmed Quantity").

    • Crucially, enter the formula: This is where you define the calculation. In your case, the formula would be:

      DELIVERY - SALES_ORDER

      (Assuming DELIVERY represents the "Referenced quantity in base unit of measure" and SALES_ORDER represents the "Confirmed Quantity". Adjust the field names to match your InfoSet.)

    • Specify the data type and length of the local field, if necessary. Usually, the query tool infers this based on the formula.

  5. Add the Local Field to the Output: Select the newly created local field (DELIVERY_VARIANCE) to be included in your report output.

  6. Execute the Query: Run the query to see the results, including the calculated delivery variance.

Example in More Detail (with potential field names):

Let's say the actual field names in your InfoSet are:

  • VBAP-KWMENG (Confirmed Quantity in Sales Order - often aliased as SALES_ORDER in the Infoset)
  • LIPS-LFIMG (Delivered Quantity / Referenced Quantity in delivery - often aliased as DELIVERY)

Then, your local field definition would be:

  • Name: DELIVERY_VARIANCE
  • Description: "Difference between Delivered and Confirmed Quantity"
  • Formula: LIPS-LFIMG - VBAP-KWMENG or DELIVERY - SALES_ORDER (depending on how the fields are aliased in your InfoSet)

Important Considerations:

  • Data Types: Ensure the data types of the fields used in the formula are compatible. You might need to use type conversions (e.g., NUMC to DEC) if necessary.
  • Aliases: If your InfoSet uses aliases for field names, use the aliases in your formula.
  • Error Handling: Consider potential errors, such as dividing by zero. You can use conditional statements (e.g., IF...THEN...ELSE) to handle these cases.

By using local fields effectively, you can create powerful and flexible reports in SAP Query without needing to modify the underlying data structure, making reporting much easier and more efficient.

entry.title: 12 Tips for Beginners Starting with SAP ABAP Programming
entry.content:
Welcome to Our SAP ABAP Programming Guide!

Are you starting your journey into the world of SAP ABAP programming? You've come to the right place! In this post, we’ve compiled 12 essential tips to help you navigate the initial steps of learning ABAP. Whether you're a complete beginner or have some basic knowledge, these tips will provide you with a solid foundation to build your skills.

12 Tips for Beginners Starting with SAP ABAP Programming

1. Understand the Basics of SAP
Before diving into ABAP, familiarize yourself with the fundamentals of SAP. Learn about SAP modules, the ERP system, and how different components interact.

2. Learn the Syntax and Structure
Start by understanding the basic syntax and structure of ABAP. Get comfortable with keywords, data types, and basic programming constructs.

3. Use the ABAP Development Tools (ADT)
Get accustomed to using ABAP Development Tools in Eclipse. It’s essential for efficient development and offers features like syntax highlighting, code completion, and debugging tools.

4. Understand Data Dictionary Objects
Learn how to work with Data Dictionary (DDIC) objects such as tables, views, data elements, and domains. These are crucial for database interactions in ABAP.

5. Practice Modularization Techniques
Master the use of subroutines, function modules, methods, and classes to write modular and reusable code. This will make your programs more maintainable and efficient.

6. Get Familiar with Internal Tables
Internal tables are a key feature in ABAP for handling data in-memory. Practice creating, populating, and manipulating internal tables.

7. Understand ALV Reports
Learn how to create and customize ALV (ABAP List Viewer) reports. ALV provides a flexible way to output data with features like sorting, filtering, and layout customization.

8. Explore Open SQL
SAP ABAP uses Open SQL for database operations. Learn the basics of SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE statements, and how to handle database transactions.

9. Practice Debugging
Get comfortable with the debugging tools in ABAP. Knowing how to effectively debug your code is crucial for identifying and fixing issues.

10. Learn About BAPIs and RFCs
BAPIs (Business Application Programming Interfaces) and RFCs (Remote Function Calls) are essential for integrating different SAP systems. Understand how to use them to enable communication between systems.

11. Get Hands-On with Enhancements and Exits
Learn about user exits, BADI (Business Add-Ins), and enhancement spots to customize and extend standard SAP functionality.

12. Engage with the SAP Community
Join the SAP community through forums, blogs, and SAP’s official website. Engaging with experienced professionals can provide valuable insights and help you stay updated with the latest developments.

entry.title: Writing Clean Code and Best Practices in SAP ABAP
entry.content:
Clean code is not just about writing code that works; it's about writing code that is readable, maintainable, and efficient. In the context of SAP ABAP, adhering to clean code principles and best practices can significantly enhance the quality of your programs. Here are some key points to keep in mind:

1. Use Meaningful Names
Choose clear, descriptive names for variables, methods, and classes. Avoid abbreviations and aim for names that convey the purpose and intent of the code.

2. Write Small and Focused Methods
Keep your methods small and focused on a single task. This makes the code easier to understand, test, and maintain. Aim for methods that do one thing and do it well.

3. Avoid Hardcoding Values
Hardcoding values makes the code inflexible and harder to maintain. Use constants or configuration tables instead, so changes can be made easily without modifying the code.

4. Comment Judiciously
Write comments that explain why a piece of code exists, not what it does. Good code should be self-explanatory, but comments can provide context and rationale for complex logic.

5. Follow Consistent Formatting
Maintain a consistent coding style and format throughout your ABAP programs. This includes indentation, spacing, and naming conventions. Consistent formatting improves readability and helps in code reviews.

6. Use Modularization Techniques
Break down complex processes into smaller, reusable components using subroutines, function modules, or methods. Modularization promotes code reuse and simplifies debugging.

7. Handle Exceptions Properly
Implement proper error handling using TRY-CATCH blocks. Ensure that your code gracefully handles exceptions and provides meaningful error messages to users.

8. Optimize Database Access
Minimize the number of database accesses and use efficient queries. Retrieve only the data you need and avoid nested SELECT statements. Use indexes and buffering where appropriate.

9. Avoid Using Obsolete Constructs
Stay updated with the latest ABAP syntax and features. Avoid using obsolete constructs and embrace modern ABAP features such as inline declarations, new string operations, and expressions.

10. Test Your Code Thoroughly
Write unit tests to validate your code's functionality. Regular testing helps catch bugs early and ensures that your code behaves as expected in different scenarios.

11. Document Your Code
Provide clear and concise documentation for your programs. This includes explaining the purpose, usage, and any special considerations of your code. Good documentation aids future maintenance and onboarding of new developers.

12. Engage in Code Reviews
Participate in code reviews to share knowledge and ensure adherence to coding standards. Peer reviews help identify potential issues and foster a culture of continuous improvement.

By following these clean code principles and best practices, you can create ABAP programs that are robust, efficient, and easy to maintain. Clean code is a key factor in the long-term success of any software project, and SAP ABAP is no exception.

entry.title: Manual Assignment of Loading and Truck Resource to FO
entry.content:
Hi All,

I would like to share my working experience on custom development for Transportation Management Optimization, SAP has already provided standard Fiori app to perform optimization, however we don't have any GUI based single step solution to perform optimization in TM.

Requirement: We received requirement from business team, to design simple SAP ABAP program to perform TM Optimization automation. based on inputs: Freight Unit, Sending Location, Receiving location, Planning Profile.

Pre-requisite: ABAP Development and BOBF Development and functional skills needed to perform above activity.

Following are the process steps:

  1. An Automatic Freight Unit is created in SAP TM, from an ERP STO document.
  2. Transportation Planning is performed and Freight Order is created from FU document.
  3. Transportation planning utilizes the optimizer engines like – TVSR/ VSR (Vehicle Resource Scheduling) and assigns a truck resource and loading resource to the Freight Order, according to the input variables from Freight Unit document.
  4. But, in case there is no loading slot available, enough to accommodate the loading activity of the Freight Order or, there is no available truck resource during the FU -> pick up window date/ time, then a manual planning is performed in the Transportation Cockpit window.
  5. But, in manual planning, only a truck resource is assigned not the loading resource.
  6. For the resolution for this business requirement, a custom manual optimization button is created, which would assign an appropriate truck resource (truck having 100% utilization for the selected FU) and a loading resource is assigned according to the prioritized sequence for the loading plant and product combination.
  7. First FO document is created against the FU, by selecting the FU and calling the standard action of the business object – TOR -> sub-node - STOP_SUCC -> action - AUTOPLAN_STAGE.
  8. Above action will create a Freight Order and assign it to the Freight Unit.
  9. Afterwards, select the newly created FO and call the action -> ASSIGN_VEHICLE_RES for assigning the truck resource at BO – TOR -> sub-node – ROOT.
  10. Once the truck resource is assigned, loading/ handling resource can be assigned to this Freight Order, with start load time, similar to FU pick up window start date/ time. Further 15 mins. of loading time is ended in start date/ time leading to load end date/ time.
  11. This would complete the manual optimization process, with a single button.

Logic: Get the all fright unit DB keys from the from the Transparent Table /SCMTMS/D_TORROT based on Document, pass all the DB keys to helper class /scmtms/cl_tor_helper_read  method GET_TOR_DATA to read Logistical Stop Successor Stage data

 

/scmtms/cl_tor_helper_read=>get_tor_data(
  EXPORTING
    it_root_key                     =  int_key                " Key Table
  IMPORTING
    et_stop_succ                   = DATA(int_stop_succ)      " Logistical Stop Successor = Stage
).

 

Using these data, get the Planning Profile data /SCMTMS/D_PLAPRO and call standard action from freight unit -> AUTOPLAN_STAGE

 

"Get instance of service manager TOR
DATA(lo_tor_srvmgr) = /bobf/cl_tra_serv_mgr_factory=>get_service_manager( /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_bo_key ).
"Get instance for transaction manager
CALL METHOD /bobf/cl_tra_trans_mgr_factory=>get_transaction_manager
  RECEIVING
    eo_transaction_manager = DATA(lo_txn_mgr). " Standalone Transaction Manager Instance
CREATE DATA lr_create_capa_param.

SELECT SINGLE * INTO (wa_plapro) FROM /scmtms/d_plapro WHERE profile_id = @p_pro.

IF sy-subrc  = 0.
  MOVE-CORRESPONDING wa_plapro TO lr_create_capa_param->plan_prof  .
ENDIF.
TRY.
    "Call action From FU -> STOP_SUCC -> AUTOPLAN_STAGE action
    lo_tor_srvmgr->do_action(
      EXPORTING
          iv_act_key           = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_action-stop_successor-autoplan_stage
          it_key               = int_fo_key
          is_parameters        = lr_create_capa_param
      IMPORTING
          eo_change                = DATA(lo_change)
          eo_message               = DATA(fo_message)
          et_failed_key            = DATA(lt_failed_key)
          et_failed_action_key     = DATA(lt_failed_action_key)
          ev_static_action_failed  = DATA(int_static_action_failed)
          et_data                  = lt_tor_root
          et_data_link             = DATA(int_data_link)
    ).
  CATCH /bobf/cx_frw_contrct_violation INTO DATA(lx_exc). " Caller violates a BOPF contract
ENDTRY.

lo_txn_mgr->save(
  IMPORTING
    ev_rejected = DATA(lv_re)            " Data element for domain BOOLE: TRUE (='X') and FALSE (=' ')
    eo_message  = fo_message                " Interface for transaction change objects
).

 

After saving the action, Now get the freight order details against freight unit /SCMTMS/D_TORITE inner join /SCMTMS/D_TORROT

 

"Get Freight Order aginst Freight Unit number
SELECT SINGLE b~db_key, b~tor_id, a~gro_vol_val, a~src_loc_keytrq, a~des_loc_keytrq
   INTO (wa_tor_fo)
   FROM /scmtms/d_torite AS a
   INNER JOIN /scmtms/d_torrot AS b
   ON a~parent_key = b~db_key
   WHERE a~fu_root_key = _tor_fu_key
   AND a~item_cat = 'PRD'
   AND b~tor_cat = 'TO'.

 

 

Assign vehicle to the freight order: Action For Assign Truck Resource to Freight Order.
If the Truck Resource ID is Available the perform the action to assign.

 

          "  action – ASSIGN_VEHICLE_RES, on Freight Order object – ROOT node, with VEH_KEY as TMSRESUID
          lo_tor_srvmgr->do_action(
            EXPORTING
              iv_act_key    = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_action-root-assign_vehicle_res
              it_key        = int_fo_key
              is_parameters = lr_assign_vehicle_res
            IMPORTING
              eo_change     = lo_change
              eo_message    = fo_message
              et_failed_key = lt_failed_key
          ).

          lo_txn_mgr->save(
            IMPORTING
              ev_rejected = lv_re            " Data element for domain BOOLE: TRUE (='X') and FALSE (=' ')
              eo_message  = fo_message       " Interface for transaction change objects
          ).

 

If The Truck Resource ID in Not Available the Perform the action which will cancel the,
Created Fright order against the Selected Freight Unit.

 

 

*-- Action to Cancle Frieght Order
          CALL METHOD lo_tor_srvmgr->do_action
            EXPORTING
              iv_act_key    = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_action-root- remove_tor_assignments
              it_key        = int_fo_key
            IMPORTING
              eo_change     = lo_change
              eo_message    = fo_message
              et_failed_key = lt_failed_key.

          lo_txn_mgr->save(
            IMPORTING
              ev_rejected = lv_re            " Data element for domain BOOLE: TRUE (='X') and FALSE (=' ')
              eo_message  = fo_message       " Interface for transaction change objects
          ).
*-- Display Message
          MESSAGE: TEXT-184 TYPE 'S' DISPLAY LIKE 'E'.
          EXIT.

 

Once the truck resource is assigned, loading/ handling resource can be assigned to this Freight Order, with start load time, similar to FU pick up window start date/ time. Further 15 mins. of loading time is ended in start date/ time leading to load end date/ time.

 

       " get all the stop and root data for freight order
        CLEAR: int_stop_key[].
        SELECT db_key INTO TABLE @int_stop_key FROM /scmtms/d_torstp WHERE parent_key = _tor_fo-db_key.

        lo_tor_srvmgr->retrieve(
          EXPORTING
            iv_node_key = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_node-stop
            it_key      = int_stop_key
          IMPORTING
            et_data     = int_fo_stop ).


        "now set the indicator based on which action we are calling
        LOOP AT int_fo_stop ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_fo_stop>) WHERE stop_cat = 'O'.
          <fs_fo_stop>-assgn_hres = wl_handling_res.
          <fs_fo_stop>-assgn_hres_cons = '1'.

          CONVERT TIME STAMP <fs_fo_stop>-aggr_assgn_start_l TIME ZONE 'UTC' INTO DATE wf_input_date TIME wf_input_time.
          wl_duration = wl_fix_dur.
          wf_hours =   wl_duration+0(2).
          wf_minutes = wl_duration+2(2).
          wf_seconds = wl_duration+4(2).

          CALL FUNCTION 'IGN_TIMESTAMP_PLUSMINUS'
            EXPORTING
              start_date  = wf_input_date
              start_time  = wf_input_time
              forward     = abap_false
              backward    = abap_true
              hours       = wf_hours
              minutes     = wf_minutes
              seconds     = wf_seconds
            IMPORTING
              result_date = wf_output_date
              result_time = wf_output_time.
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            <fs_fo_stop>-aggr_assgn_start_l = wf_output_date && wf_output_time.
          ENDIF.
        ENDLOOP.

        INSERT /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_node_attribute-stop-assgn_hres      INTO TABLE int_chg_fields.
        INSERT /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_node_attribute-stop-assgn_hres_cons INTO TABLE int_chg_fields.
        INSERT /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_node_attribute-stop-aggr_assgn_start_l INTO TABLE int_chg_fields.

        /scmtms/cl_mod_helper=>mod_update_multi(
          EXPORTING
            it_data           = int_fo_stop
            iv_node           = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_node-stop
            iv_bo_key         = /scmtms/if_tor_c=>sc_bo_key
            it_changed_fields = int_chg_fields
          CHANGING
            ct_mod            = int_mod ).

        lo_tor_srvmgr->modify(
          EXPORTING
            it_modification = int_mod ).

        lo_txn_mgr->save(
          IMPORTING
            ev_rejected = lv_re            " Data element for domain BOOLE: TRUE (='X') and FALSE (=' ')
            eo_message  = fo_message       " Interface for transaction change objects
        ).

 

Display success message from the standard messages.

entry.title: ABAP LLM Client Beta Release
entry.content:
Following the alpha preview announcement, I'm excited to share that ABAP LLM Client has reached its beta milestone with version 0.1.0. This release brings several significant improvements and new features that make LLM integration in ABAP easier to use.

What's New in Beta

Enhanced Provider Support

  • Added Azure OpenAI Services support
  • Existing integrations with OpenAI, OLLAMA, and OpenRouter improved

Improved Security

  • Implemented SSF-based secret encryption
  • New BAdI interfaces for custom security implementations, authorization checks, and more
  • Flexible architecture allowing easy integration of your security requirements

Tool (Function call) Support

  • Added support for tool calls
  • Enhanced configuration options

Developer Experience

  • Streamlined API design for more intuitive usage
  • Reduced complexity 
  • Documentation for quick start

Looking Ahead

While this beta release marks a significant milestone, it's important to note that this client serves as a foundation for more exciting developments to come.

Getting Started

Visit the GitHub repository to:

  • Access installation instructions
  • Review documentation
  • Explore example implementations
  • Provide feedback or report issues

Stay tuned for upcoming announcements about new projects building upon this foundation!

entry.title: CDS Views 7.5x Quick Reference
entry.content:
Introduction

This Quick Reference is a collection of new CDS functionality made available in ABAP 7.5x releases. This functionality is summarised in a way that will make sense to those familiar with SAP CDS. Links to the SAP Help  are also provided throughout to allow the reader to dive in deeper if desired. The version the functionality was released is also given to aid in determining if it is available in your system.

 

S/4HANA151116101709180919092020202120222023
ABAP7.507.517.527.537.547.557.567.577.58

Contents

  1. CDS View Entity

  2. CDS Abstract Entity
  3. CDS Custom Entity
  4. CDS Projection views

 

1. CDS View Entity (7.50)

Syntax

[@enity_annot1]
[@entity_annot2]
...
[@view_entity_annot1]
[@view_entity_annot2]
...
[DEFINE] [ROOT] VIEW ENTITY view_entity
        [parameter_list]
         AS select_statement [;]...

Description

CDS view entities are non DDIC-based views. While they were introduced in 7.50 the old DDIC-based views (DEFINE VIEW ddic_based_view) only became obsolete in 7.56.

 

Extensions

EXTEND VIEW ENTITY (7.55)

Makes it possible to add new view fields to existing CDS view entities and CDS projection views by using CDS view entity extensions.

EXTEND VIEW ENTITY cds_view_entity | projection_view
       WITH
            [association1
             association2 ...]
            { select_list_extension }
            [GROUP BY field1, field2, ...  ]
            [EXCEPT | INTERSECT | UNION [ALL]  { ... }] [;]

 

ANNOTATE ENTITY (7.53)

Define CDS metadata extensions(type:DDLX) for any CDS entities with the exception of CDS table functions.

@Metadata.layer: #CORE|#LOCALIZATION|#INDUSTRY|#PARTNER|#CUSTOMER
   [@entity_annot1]
   [@entity_annot2]
   ...
   ANNOTATE ENTITY cds_entity WITH
     [ parameter_list]
     { [element_list] }[;]

 

Set Operators

UNION (7.56)       

Now supported in CDS view entities.

EXCEPT (7.56) 

Returns all rows of a SELECT statement that are not part of the result sets of the following SELECT statements.

INTERSECT (7.56)

Returns all distinct rows that are included in all result sets of multiple SELECT statements.

 

Functions

GET_NUMERIC_VALUE (7.56)

Conversion Function. Returns the numeric value of a currency or quantity without its currency or unit key.

 

 

 

  key carrid,
  key connid,
  key fldate,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency'
      price,
      currency,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency'
      paymentsum,
      GET_NUMERIC_VALUE( paymentsum )  / GET_NUMERIC_VALUE( price )
                as number_of_bookings

 

 

 

CURR_TO_DECFLOAT_AMOUNT (7.56)

 Conversion Function. Converts the function argument(must have data type CURR and a currency key ) into a type DECFLOAT34.

 

 

 

  key id,
      cuky                              as currency,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency'
      curr10_4                          as amount,
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'currency'
      CURR_TO_DECFLOAT_AMOUNT(curr10_4) as curr_conv

 

 

 

SUBSTRING (7.57)

Function enhanced to accept literals, fields, parameters, expressions, and built-in functions as arguments pos and len.

SUBSTRING(arg, pos, len)     Substring arg from the position pos with length len. 

 

LEFT and RIGHT (7.57)

Functions enhanced to accept  literals, fields, parameters, expressions, and built-in functions as argument len.

LEFT(arg, len)                String of the length len with the len left characters of arg (ignoring the trailing blanks).

RIGHT(arg,len)              String of the length len with the len right characters of arg (ignoring the trailing blanks).

 

Types

Typed Literals (7.56) 

Now supported in CDS view entities. Not supported in the ON condition of a CDS association.

 

 

 

{
  key id,
      abap.char'aaa   '       as char_trailing_blanks,
      abap.sstring'aaa   '  as sstring_trailing_blanks,

      abap.string'aaa   '    as string_trailing_blanks,
      'aaa'                               as untyped_literal   }
where
  numc1 = abap.numc'1' and
   datn < abap.datn'20200101'

 

 

 

CDS Calculated Quantity (7.56)

A new type that is the result of a calculation using amount and/or quantity fields.

 

Application Session Variables (7.57)

In form $session.vname

vname

Value when accessed

bs_instance_id

Instance ID of the current business service instance. The returned value has the data type SSTRING.

bs_zone_id

Zone ID of the current business service instance. The returned value has the data type SSTRING.

 

Input Parameters (7.57)

abap.string now supported.

 

ABAP Reports

RUTDDLS_MIGRATION_CANDIDATES (7.56) 

Evaluates whether a migration from a CDS DDIC-based view to a CDS view entity is possible.

 RUTDDLSV2MIGRATION (7.56) 

Migrates CDS DDIC-based views to CDS view entities.

RUT_WHERE_USE_SQLVIEW (7.57)

Lists all repository objects that use CDS-managed DDIC views.

 

Annotations

Table Buffering Annotation (7.57).

Defines whether table buffering is allowed for a CDS view entity or not.

@AbapCatalog.entityBuffer.definitionAllowed: true|false

 

CDS entity buffer (7.57)

The actual buffering type can be defined by a separate ABAP tuning object called a CDS entity buffer with the DDL statement:

DEFINE VIEW ENTITY BUFFER ON cds_view_entity
                   LAYER CORE
                       | LOCALIZATION
                       | INDUSTRY
                       | PARTNER
                       | CUSTOMER
                   TYPE SINGLE
                       | {GENERIC NUMBER OF KEY ELEMENTS number}
                       | FULL
                       | NONE

Table buffering can be defined differently for the layers core, localization, industry, partner and customer.

 

Other

DISTINCT (7.56)

Available for SELECT statements in CDS view entities. Works like ABAP SELECT.

 

Reusing Expressions (7.56)

Reuse expressions defined in the SELECT list in other operand positions of the same CDS view entity.

 

 

 

key id                                 as field1,
    concat($projection.field1, 'x')    as field_reuse,

 

 

 

WHERE clause (7.56)

Now supports arithmetic expressions and case expressions as operands.

 

 

 

define view entity DEMO_SALES_ORDER_WHERE
  as select from
    demo_sales_order
    association to demo_sales_bupa as _partner
      on $projection.id = _partner.id
    {
      key so_key,
          id,
          company_code,
          _partner.family_name
    }
    where
          _partner.family_name like    'S%'
      and length( _partner.family_name ) = abap.int1'4'
      and created_on between abap.dats'20200101' and abap.dats'20200401'
      and cast( _partner.birth_name as DEMO_BT_BIRTH_NAME
                preserving type )  =       abap.char'Meier'

 

 

 

 HAVING clause (7.56)

Now supports arithmetic expressions and case expressions as operands.

 

 

 

define view entity DEMO_CDS_VIEW_ENTITY_HAVING
  as select from sflight
  {
    concat_with_space(carrid, connid, 1) as ID,
    count(*)                             as col_count,
    seatsmax
  }
  group by
    carrid,
    connid,
    seatsmax
  having
    seatsmax > abap.int2'500'

 

 

 

Searched Case Expression (7.56)

Now supports arithmetic expressions and case expressions as operands.

 

 

 

define view entity demo_cds_searched_case_ve
  as select from
    spfli
    {
      key carrid,
      key connid,
          distance,
          distid,
          case
            when distance >= 2000 then 'long-haul flight'
            when distance >= 1000 and
                 distance <  2000 then 'medium-haul flight'
            when distance <  1000 then 'short-haul flight'
                                  else 'error'
          end as flight_type
    }
    where
      distid = 'MI'

 

 

 

2. CDS Abstract Entity (7.53)

Syntax

[@entity_annot1]
[@entity_annot2]
...
[DEFINE] [ROOT] ABSTRACT ENTITY abstract_entity
         [parameter_list]
          element_list[;]

Description

Works like a structure definition but nothing is created in DDIC and has annotations that can be evaluated with class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_SERVICE.

 

EXTEND ABSTRACT ENTITY (7.56)

Allows extension of existing abstract entity

EXTEND ABSTRACT ENTITY cds_abstract_entity WITH  { element_list_extension }

 

To-Parent Association Without ON (7.56) 

The ON condition can be left out of a “to-parent” if the association target is also a CDS abstract entity.

 

 

 

define abstract entity DEMO_CDS_ABSTRACT_CHILD
{
  key key_field  : abap.char(1);
      data_field    : abap.int4;
      _to_parent  : association to parent DEMO_CDS_ABSTRACT_COMPOSITION  
                     on _to_parent.key_field = $projection.key_field;
           <- Last line can be omitted
}

 

 

 

 3. CDS Custom Entity (7.54)

Syntax

[@entity_annot1]
[@entity_annot2]
...
[@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy : 'class_name']
[DEFINE] [ROOT] CUSTOM ENTITY custom_entity
         [parameter_list]
          element_list[;]

Description

No DDIC entries created and has annotations that can be evaluated with class CL_DD_DDL_ANNOTATION_SERVICE.

Used in RAP as basis of unmanaged queries.

 

 EXTEND CUSTOM ENTITY (7.57)

Makes it possible to add new elements to existing CDS custom entities by using CDS custom entity extensions.

EXTEND CUSTOM ENTITY CustomEntity WITH { ElementListExtension }

 

4. CDS Projection Views

Syntax

[@entity_annot1]
[@entity_annot2]
...
[@proj_view_annot1]
[@proj_view_annot2]
...
DEFINE [ROOT] VIEW ENTITY projection_view
   [ PROVIDER CONTRACT provider_contract]               [From 7.56]
    AS PROJECTION ON cds_entity [AS alias_name]
   [association1 association2 ...]                                 [From 7.55]
   [redefined_assoc1 redefined_assoc2 ...]                    [From 7.56]
      { projection_list }
   [WHERE cds_cond]                                              [From 7.58 for Transactional Interfaces ]

Description

A special CDS view entity that is based on another CDS view entity (called projected entity). CDS projection views adapt a CDS data model for service-specific use cases.

 

Associations (7.55)

It is possible to add new, local, read-only associations association1, association2 to a projection view. Read-only associations in projection views can only be exposed but they cannot be used in path expressions to expose elements from new associations.

 

REDEFINE ASSOCIATION (7.56) 

For Projection views it is now possible to redefine an association. In this way, it is possible to modify an association from the underlying view. 

... REDEFINE ASSOCIATION [source.]_ProjAssoc [ filter] [AS _RedefinedName] redirection  ...

 

Provider Contracts

PROVIDER CONTRACT (7.56) 

For Projection views it is now possible to have a provider contract which specifies the scenario in which a CDS projection view is used.  Provider contract options available:

  • TRANSACTIONAL_QUERY (7.56)             [Default if not provided]
  • TRANSACTIONAL_INTERFACE (7.57)      [Use for Transactional Interfaces]
  • ANALYTICAL_QUERY (7.57)                     [Use for Transient View Entities]

 

CDS Transactional Query (7.56)

Uses DEFINE [ROOT] VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON  with  provider contract “TRANSACTIONAL_QUERY”.

Transactional queries are intended for modelling the projection layer of a RAP business object. A transactional projection view must always be part of a business object. That means it must either be a root entity itself, or it must be part of a composition tree that contains a root entity

 

CDS Transactional Interface (7.57)

Uses DEFINE VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON  with  provider contract “TRANSACTIONAL_INTERFACE”.

CDS transactional interfaces serve as stable public interface layer in a CDS data model. They are typically used in the context of the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model to provide the basis for a RAP BO interface. 

WHERE  (7.58) condition allowed for Transactional Interfaces.

 

CDS Analytical Projection Views (7.57)

Uses DEFINE TRANSIENT VIEW ENTITY AS PROJECTION ON  with provider contract “ANALYTICAL_QUERY”.

A CDS DDL that is intended for modeling analytical queries within a CDS data model. A CDS analytical projection view defines an analytical query in its element_list.

The projected entity cds_entity can be an analytical cube view(7.57) or an analytical dimension view(7.58). One base view can serve as a basis for multiple analytical projection views.

entry.title: How to implement "readonly : update" for draft instances
entry.content:
Introduction

Via the customer influence channel the requirement was raised to "allow to modify a field for a draft entity that is not already created in persistency table, the record only exists in draft table." when the field has been configured as readonly : update in the behavior definition.  

Since this does not work out of the box, I will show how such a behavior, a field which is read-only after the first draft has been saved can be achieved in RAP using the SAP standard. 

  

Problem description 

When a field is being defined as readonly for update  

 

 

  field ( readonly : update ) AgencyId;

 

 

and if this application is draft-enabled it turns out, that the field cannot be edited after having provided an intial value in the first dialogue of your application. 

Solution ( implementation ) 

You can test this code with sample RAP business object that is being created using the following tutorial:  

Build an SAP Fiori elements App Using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) – Beginner [RAP100] | SAP Tutorials

The desired behavior can be achieved by using dynamic feature control. Instead of using the statement readonly : update you can use the following syntax in your behavior defintion.  

 

 

 field ( features : instance ) AgencyId;

 

 

And in your behavior implementation in the get_instance_features() method you have to implement code that checks if an active instance of the travel entity is available. 

If yes, the field will be made readonly. If not, and this is only the case when the object is being created as a draft but has not been persisted yet, the field will still be editable.  

 

 

  METHOD get_instance_features.

    DATA result_line LIKE LINE OF result.

    " read relevant travel instance data
    READ ENTITIES OF zrap100_r_traveltp_rou IN LOCAL MODE
      ENTITY Travel
        FIELDS ( AgencyId )
        WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys )
      RESULT DATA(travels)
      FAILED failed.

    LOOP AT travels INTO DATA(travel).

      SELECT SINGLE * FROM zrap100_r_traveltp_rou
                      WHERE TravelId = -TravelId
                      INTO (travel_cds_line).

      result_line-%tky = travel-%tky.

      IF travel_cds_line IS NOT INITIAL.
        result_line-%features-%field-AgencyId =  if_abap_behv=>fc-f-read_only .
      ELSE.
        result_line-%features-%field-AgencyId = if_abap_behv=>fc-f-unrestricted.
      ENDIF.

      APPEND result_line TO result.

    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

 

 

 

Result

The app now behaves as follows: 

  • We create a new travel instance.
    1. We enter a value for the field AgencyId
    2. and press the "Back" button


 

  • We can now see the newly created instance in our drafts
    1. We select the editing status "Own Draft"   
    2. We select the newly created entity   



 

  • When we now edit the entity again we see that the field AgencyId can still be edited
    1. We press Create to persist the data.




The newly created item shows up and we press Edit again




  • Finally we can see that the field AgencyId has become readonly because an active instance exists.

entry.title: New SAP Discovery Center Mission: Mastering SAP S/4HANA Extension with SAP Build
entry.content:
From the very beginning, SAP Build has been introduced as a unified low-code platform, enabling developers of all skill levels to create, extend, and automate business applications with drag-and-drop simplicity. Later, its capabilities were expanded with the integration of Java and JavaScript development, enhanced by AI features. And now the latest enhancements of SAP Build include ABAP development capabilities, advanced generative AI features, and deeper integration with SAP S/4HANA and other business applications.
 
SAP Build simplifies extension development and accelerates ERP modernization by seamlessly connecting all SAP development tools — ABAP, Java, JavaScript, and low-code — to enable effortless collaboration across diverse skill sets.
 
Start the new mission Mastering SAP S/4HANA Extension with SAP Build to try out the power of the Fusion Development experience provided by SAP Build.
 

Who is the mission made for?

 
No matter if you are a professional developer or a citizen developer with small development experience, this mission will guide you to the process of developing your extension application within a few hours.

What is it about?

 
The tutorial scenario is based on the following story:
 
Suzan, a medical technician at SBA Medical Clinic, noticed a small issue with one of the medical devices during her routine equipment checks. To address it quickly, she used the Service Hub Client mobile app to report the problem directly from her smartphone. The app, powered by SAP's Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP), immediately logged the incident in a central system, ensuring all service details were captured in one place.
 

 

Alex, the service manager at Health Tech Devices, received the report through the Service Hub Admin interface. He quickly reviewed the issue, prioritized it, and assigned a technician to fix the problem. Using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming model (RAP) power in background, Alex is able to assign the nearest processor of the issue, seeing all available technicians on the map within a selected range.
 

 

What will I do?

 
The final solution consists of several services and applications driven by SAP BTP.
 
 
You will start development by creating and deploying a CAP application using SAP Build Code. This application will be the heart of the project, processing data for equipment maintenance issues. All the steps will be done using the power of artificial intelligence provided by Joule. You will design a data model, expose it with the services, implement some custom logic and deploy to the Cloud Foundry environment, including SAP HANA artifacts.
 
 
You will then create another background service using SAP BTP ABAP environment. This will provide the data for the available incident processors. The data includes some processor information and geo-location. The main data of a processor is assumed to come from an SAP S/4HANA Cloud system (Business Partner data). For simplicity, this data is uploaded only once. In real production scenarios, there should be a more comprehensive integration. To reproduce this scenario, you don't need an access to a real SAP S/4HANA Cloud system as you will use the SAP Business Accelerator Hub Sandbox. In addition, you will create a service using the ABAP RESTful Application Programming model to search for the processors within a given distance range using the power of the SAP HANA database geo-engine.
 
 
To access the data from your front-end applications, you will create SAP BTP destinations.
 
When all background services are available you will use SAP Build Apps to create 2 cross-platform applications. You will use all the power of SAP Build Apps hidden behind the drag-and-drop simplicity, including advanced components, complex (but still intuitive) formulas, REST-based integration for non-standard data requests, and so on.
 

How can I start?

 
Try out the mission in SAP Discovery Center for free. The content of the mission is tested on a regular basis and will be kept up to date by integrating the latest services and features.
 
Even though this mission involves numerous services and applications, you can complete all the steps from start to finish using the SAP BTP Trial Account.
 
You are interested in more end-to-end missions? Pick up one of them from SAP Discovery Center.
 
We are excited to hear your feedback about this mission. Please feel free share your thoughts in the comment section below or reach out to us via Discovery Center support tool.

entry.title: REUSE_ALV_GRID_DISPLAY - Dynamic Total in Editable ALV
entry.content:
In SAP ABAP, handling editable ALV (ABAP List Viewer) grids with dynamic events such as data changes and automatic summations can be tricky, but it provides a very effective way to interact with and manipulate data. The program we are reviewing here aims to achieve just that—enable editable fields in the ALV grid, track changes in those fields, and update the summation of those changes at runtime.

The main objective of the blog is to create an editable ALV grid where the user can modify the weight (BRGEW) of materials (MATNR). When any change occurs, the summation of the BRGEW field is automatically recalculated and displayed.

The ALV grid provides a user-friendly interface for displaying the data, and we utilize event handling (specifically the DATA_CHANGED event) to capture and process changes made by the user. Additionally, since the Enter key does not trigger a default action in the ALV grid, the report uses the DATA_CHANGED event to monitor any updates and recalculate the summation.

Key Concepts:

  • Editable ALV: The ALV grid is made editable for the field BRGEW.
  • Event Handling: The DATA_CHANGED event is used to detect when data in the grid changes, which triggers the summation update.
  • Summation: Whenever the user edits the BRGEW field, the program recalculates the total value of the BRGEW column and displays the updated sum.

Code Snippet:

 

REPORT ztest7.
TYPE-POOLS:slis.

DATA: gt_mara     TYPE TABLE OF ztest_s,
      gt_fieldcat TYPE slis_t_fieldcat_alv,
      gt_events   TYPE slis_t_event,
      go_grid     TYPE REF TO cl_gui_alv_grid,
      gs_stable   TYPE lvc_s_stbl,
      gr_data     TYPE REF TO data.

FIELD-SYMBOLS: <gtr_sum_tab> TYPE table.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  SELECT matnr,
         brgew
    FROM mara
    INTO TABLE _mara UP TO 10 ROWS.
  IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
    CALL FUNCTION 'REUSE_ALV_FIELDCATALOG_MERGE'
      EXPORTING
        i_program_name         = sy-repid
        i_structure_name       = 'ZTEST_S'
      CHANGING
        ct_fieldcat            = gt_fieldcat
      EXCEPTIONS
        inconsistent_interface = 1
        program_error          = 2
        OTHERS                 = 3.
    IF sy-subrc = 0.
      READ TABLE gt_fieldcat ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_fcat>) WITH KEY fieldname = 'BRGEW'.
      IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
        <gs_fcat>-edit = 'X'.
        <gs_fcat>-do_sum = 'X'.
      ENDIF.

      APPEND VALUE #( name = 'DATA_CHANGED' form = 'GET_DATA_CHANGED' ) TO gt_events.

      CALL FUNCTION 'REUSE_ALV_GRID_DISPLAY'
        EXPORTING
          i_callback_program       = sy-repid
          it_fieldcat              = gt_fieldcat
          it_events                = gt_events
          i_callback_pf_status_set = 'SET_PF_STATUS'
        TABLES
          t_outtab                 = gt_mara.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.


FORM set_pf_status USING itr_extab TYPE slis_t_extab.

* Get the ALV Object reference
  CALL FUNCTION 'GET_GLOBALS_FROM_SLVC_FULLSCR'
    IMPORTING
      e_grid = go_grid.
  IF go_grid IS BOUND.
    CALL METHOD go_grid->register_edit_event
      EXPORTING
        i_event_id = cl_gui_alv_grid=>mc_evt_modified.

    CALL METHOD go_grid->register_edit_event
      EXPORTING
        i_event_id = cl_gui_alv_grid=>mc_evt_enter.
  ENDIF.

  " PF Status Code
  CASE sy-ucomm.
*	WHEN
    WHEN OTHERS.
  ENDCASE.
ENDFORM.

FORM get_data_changed USING io_data_changed TYPE REF TO cl_alv_changed_data_protocol.

  IF go_grid IS BOUND.

    LOOP AT io_data_changed->mt_mod_cells ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_changed>) WHERE fieldname = 'BRGEW'.
      READ TABLE gt_mara ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_tab>) INDEX <gs_changed>-row_id.
      IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
        <gs_tab>-brgew = <gs_changed>-value.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.

    go_grid->get_subtotals(
      IMPORTING
        ep_collect00 = gr_data   " Overall Total
    ).

    ASSIGN gr_data->* TO <gtr_sum_tab>.
    READ TABLE <gtr_sum_tab> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<l_sum>) INDEX 1.
    IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'BRGEW' OF STRUCTURE <l_sum> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lg_val>).
      IF <lg_val> IS ASSIGNED.
        <lg_val> = REDUCE #( INIT lv_i TYPE ntgew_15 FOR <ls_t> IN gt_mara NEXT lv_i = lv_i + <ls_t>-brgew ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

    gs_stable-col = 'X'.
    gs_stable-row = 'X'.

    go_grid->refresh_table_display(
      EXPORTING
        is_stable      = gs_stable      " With Stable Rows/Columns
        i_soft_refresh = 'X'            " Without Sort, Filter, etc.
      EXCEPTIONS
        finished       = 1              " Display was Ended (by Export)
        OTHERS         = 2
    ).
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    ENDIF.

  ENDIF.

ENDFORM.                    "get_data_changed

 

Event Handler Registration

 

APPEND VALUE #( name = 'DATA_CHANGED' form = 'GET_DATA_CHANGED' ) TO gt_events.

 

The DATA_CHANGED event is added to the event table (gt_events). This event will trigger the GET_DATA_CHANGED form when any modification happens to the grid.

Make the BRGEW Field Editable and Enable Summation

 

READ TABLE gt_fieldcat ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_fcat>) WITH KEY fieldname = 'BRGEW'.
IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
  <gs_fcat>-edit = 'X'.
  <gs_fcat>-do_sum = 'X'.
ENDIF.

 

In this section, we search for the BRGEW field in the field catalog (gt_fieldcat). If the field is found, it is set to be editable (edit = 'X') and summable (do_sum = 'X').

PF Status

 

FORM set_pf_status USING itr_extab TYPE slis_t_extab.
  " Get the ALV Object reference
  CALL FUNCTION 'GET_GLOBALS_FROM_SLVC_FULLSCR'
    IMPORTING
      e_grid = go_grid.
  IF go_grid IS BOUND.
    CALL METHOD go_grid->register_edit_event
      EXPORTING
        i_event_id = cl_gui_alv_grid=>mc_evt_modified.
    CALL METHOD go_grid->register_edit_event
      EXPORTING
        i_event_id = cl_gui_alv_grid=>mc_evt_enter.
  ENDIF.
  " PF Status Code
  CASE sy-ucomm.
    WHEN OTHERS.
  ENDCASE.
ENDFORM.

 

Intentionally, the FM GET_GLOBALS_FROM_SLVC_FULLSCR is initialized, and events are registered in the PF status so that the ENTER key press event should be detected. This can also be added in the TOP-OF-PAGE event. The ABAP code retrieves an ALV grid object (go_grid) using the function GET_GLOBALS_FROM_SLVC_FULLSCR and checks if it is properly initialized with the IS BOUND check. If the grid object is bound, it registers two edit events for the grid: one for when the user modifies data (mc_evt_modified), and another for when the user presses the Enter key (mc_evt_enter). These events are linked to the grid to allow further processing or actions in response to user interactions with the grid, such as triggering updates or executing custom logic.

Data Changed Handler (Recalculate Sum)

 

FORM get_data_changed USING io_data_changed TYPE REF TO cl_alv_changed_data_protocol.
  IF go_grid IS BOUND.
    LOOP AT io_data_changed->mt_mod_cells ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_changed>) WHERE fieldname = 'BRGEW'.
      READ TABLE gt_mara ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<gs_tab>) INDEX <gs_changed>-row_id.
      IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
        <gs_tab>-brgew = <gs_changed>-value.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
    go_grid->get_subtotals(
      IMPORTING
        ep_collect00 = gr_data   " Overall Total
    ).
    ASSIGN gr_data->* TO <gtr_sum_tab>.
    READ TABLE <gtr_sum_tab> ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<l_sum>) INDEX 1.
    IF sy-subrc EQ 0.
      ASSIGN COMPONENT 'BRGEW' OF STRUCTURE <l_sum> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<lg_val>).
      IF <lg_val> IS ASSIGNED.
        <lg_val> = REDUCE #( INIT lv_i TYPE ntgew_15 FOR <ls_t> IN gt_mara NEXT lv_i = lv_i + <ls_t>-brgew ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.
    gs_stable-col = 'X'.
    gs_stable-row = 'X'.
    go_grid->refresh_table_display(
      EXPORTING
        is_stable      = gs_stable      " With Stable Rows/Columns
        i_soft_refresh = 'X'            " Without Sort, Filter, etc.
      EXCEPTIONS
        finished       = 1              " Display was Ended (by Export)
        OTHERS         = 2
    ).
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
    ENDIF.
  ENDIF.
ENDFORM.

 

In the get_data_changed form, the system loops through the modified cells, retrieves the updated value, and updates the corresponding entry in the internal table gt_mara. Then, it calculates the sum of the BRGEW column and refreshes the ALV grid to display the updated total.

In conclusion, this blog demonstrates how to build an interactive and editable ALV grid in SAP ABAP that responds to user modifications in real-time. By leveraging the DATA_CHANGED event, we are able to efficiently track and process changes to the data, recalculate sums dynamically, and display the updated results without requiring the user to press any additional buttons. This approach provides a seamless and intuitive user experience while utilizing SAP's built-in functionality to manage the ALV grid and event handling.

Thanks for Reading!

I hope this blog post provided useful insights into creating dynamic, editable ALV grids in SAP. Feel free to adapt and customize the provided code to suit your specific needs.

Regards,
Abhijeet Tikar

entry.title: Disenchantment: GPT for generating ABAP code documentation
entry.content:
I just asked a publicly GPT trained model at my company, to generate the documentation of an old and huge ABAP program, to see if ChatGPT can save me some time to understand what the program does.

The prompt is limited to one or two thousand characters, so I can only ask for one portion of a subroutine at a time. The generated text is just good for explaining the meaning of each individual ABAP statement. It cannot save time.

Below are two questions and answers for your information.

Did you have a better experience with GenAI for generating a good ABAP documentation?

Which engine do you use? (public, SAP Joule, privately trained model, etc.)

Thank you.

Sandra

First prompt:

Describe the detailed steps of the subroutine SAVE_COMMENT. Here is its code: FORM save_comment USING p_vl_max_fake p_ok_boolean. CLEAR sg_zfake_user. MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_2. IF gw_mode = 'CREER'. MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_1. MOVE 'E' TO sg_zfake_user-statut. ELSE. DELETE FROM zfake_user WHERE nfake = gi_nfake AND typefake = '2'. IF sy-subrc <> 0. ENDIF. MOVE g_date_1 TO sg_zfake_user-date_1. MOVE g_statut TO sg_zfake_user-statut. ENDIF. MOVE gi_nfake TO sg_zfake_user-nfake. MOVE '2' TO sg_zfake_user-typefake. MOVE 0 TO sg_zfake_user-notypefake. PERFORM affichage_commentaire USING 'GET' tg_fake_commentaire_user_m tg_fake_commentaire_admin_m. LOOP AT tg_fake_commentaire_user_m INTO sg_fake_commentaire. CONCATENATE sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 sg_fake_commentaire INTO sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 SEPARATED BY space. ENDLOOP. CONDENSE sg_zfake_user-rubrique1. MOVE sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 TO g_rubrique1. LOOP AT tg_fake_commentaire_admin_m INTO sg_fake_commentaire. CONCATENATE sg_zfake_user-rubrique2 sg_fake_commentaire INTO sg_zfake_user-rubrique2 SEPARATED BY space. ENDLOOP. CONDENSE sg_zfake_user-rubrique2. MOVE sg_zfake_user-rubrique2 TO g_rubrique2. MOVE p_encopiede TO sg_zfake_user-rubrique3. IF p_encopiede IS NOT INITIAL. CLEAR : b_mdp_ecc, b_sup_ecc , b_sup_decisionnel, sg_zfake_user-rubrique4, sg_zfake_user-rubrique6, sg_zfake_user-rubrique7. ELSE. IF b_mdp_ecc = 'X'. MOVE 'X' TO sg_zfake_user-rubrique4. ENDIF. IF b_mdp_infocentre = 'X'. MOVE 'X' TO sg_zfake_user-rubrique5. ENDIF. IF b_sup_ecc = 'X'. MOVE 'X' TO sg_zfake_user-rubrique6. ENDIF. IF b_sup_decisionnel = 'X'. MOVE 'X' TO sg_zfake_user-rubrique7. ENDIF. ENDIF. INSERT zfake_user FROM sg_zfake_user. IF sy-subrc <> 0. UPDATE zfake_user FROM sg_zfake_user. ENDIF. IF sy-subrc <> 0. MESSAGE 'Cannot save the comments' TYPE 'S' p_ok_boolean = 'X'. EXIT. ENDIF. ENDFORM.

The answer:

There’s a problem processing your request. Please try again later or let me know if I can help.

I shortened the prompt:

Describe the detailed steps of the subroutine SAVE_COMMENT. Here is its code: FORM save_comment USING p_vl_max_fake p_ok_boolean. CLEAR sg_zfake_user. MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_2. IF gw_mode = 'CREER'. MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_1. MOVE 'E' TO sg_zfake_user-statut. ELSE. DELETE FROM zfake_user WHERE nfake = gi_nfake AND typefake = '2'. IF sy-subrc <> 0. ENDIF. MOVE g_date_1 TO sg_zfake_user-date_1. MOVE g_statut TO sg_zfake_user-statut. ENDIF. MOVE gi_nfake TO sg_zfake_user-nfake. MOVE '2' TO sg_zfake_user-typefake. MOVE 0 TO sg_zfake_user-notypefake. PERFORM affichage_commentaire USING 'GET' tg_fake_commentaire_user_m tg_fake_commentaire_admin_m. LOOP AT tg_fake_commentaire_user_m INTO sg_fake_commentaire. CONCATENATE sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 sg_fake_commentaire INTO sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 SEPARATED BY space. ENDLOOP.

The answer:

Using publicly trained GPT model...

Sure, let's break down the subroutine `SAVE_COMMENT` step by step:

Subroutine Definition
FORM save_comment USING p_vl_max_fake p_ok_boolean.
The subroutine SAVE_COMMENT is defined with two parameters: p_vl_max_fake and p_ok_boolean.

Step 1: Clear the Work Area
CLEAR sg_zfake_user.
The work area sg_zfake_user is cleared to ensure it starts with no data.

Step 2: Set the Current Date
MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_2.
The current system date (sy-datum) is moved to the field date_2 of the work area sg_zfake_user.

Step 3: Check the Mode
IF gw_mode = 'CREER'.
The subroutine checks if the mode (gw_mode) is 'CREER' (which likely means 'create').

Step 3.1: If Mode is 'CREER'
MOVE sy-datum TO sg_zfake_user-date_1.
MOVE 'E' TO sg_zfake_user-statut.
The current system date (sy-datum) is moved to the field date_1 of the work area sg_zfake_user.
The status field statut of the work area sg_zfake_user is set to 'E'.

Step 3.2: If Mode is Not 'CREER'
ELSE.
DELETE FROM zfake_user WHERE nfake = gi_nfake AND typefake = '2'.
IF sy-subrc <> 0.
ENDIF.
MOVE g_date_1 TO sg_zfake_user-date_1.
MOVE g_statut TO sg_zfake_user-statut.
The subroutine deletes entries from the database table zfake_user where nfake equals gi_nfake and typefake equals '2'.
If the deletion is not successful (sy-subrc is not zero), it does nothing (empty ENDIF).
The value of g_date_1 is moved to the field date_1 of the work area sg_zfake_user.
The value of g_statut is moved to the field statut of the work area sg_zfake_user.

Step 4: Set Additional Fields
MOVE gi_nfake TO sg_zfake_user-nfake.
MOVE '2' TO sg_zfake_user-typefake.
MOVE 0 TO sg_zfake_user-notypefake.
The value of gi_nfake is moved to the field nfake of the work area sg_zfake_user.
The value '2' is moved to the field typefake of the work area sg_zfake_user.
The value 0 is moved to the field notypefake of the work area sg_zfake_user.

Step 5: Perform Another Subroutine
PERFORM affichage_commentaire USING 'GET' tg_fake_commentaire_user_m tg_fake_commentaire_admin_m.
The subroutine affichage_commentaire is called with the parameters 'GET', tg_fake_commentaire_user_m, and tg_fake_commentaire_admin_m.

Step 6: Loop Through Internal Table
LOOP AT tg_fake_commentaire_user_m INTO sg_fake_commentaire.
CONCATENATE sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 sg_fake_commentaire INTO sg_zfake_user-rubrique1 SEPARATED BY space.
ENDLOOP.
The subroutine loops through the internal table tg_fake_commentaire_user_m and reads each entry into the work area sg_fake_commentaire.
For each entry, it concatenates the value of sg_fake_commentaire to the field rubrique1 of the work area sg_zfake_user, separated by a space.

End of Subroutine
ENDFORM.
The subroutine ends.

Summary
The SAVE_COMMENT subroutine performs the following actions:

  • Clears the work area sg_zfake_user.
  • Sets the current date in date_2.
  • Depending on the mode (gw_mode), it either sets the creation date and status or deletes existing entries and sets the date and status from global variables.
  • Sets additional fields in the work area.
  • Calls another subroutine affichage_commentaire.
  • Loops through an internal table and concatenates its entries to a field in the work area.

entry.title: Date to Factory Date Conversion with S/4HANA ABAP CDS view
entry.content:
I wish S/4HANA ABAP CDS views supported date-to-factory-date conversion using an out-of-the-box built-in function, similar to currency or unit conversion. Unfortunately, that’s not the case. But don’t worry—I’ve got you covered with a custom-built S/4HANA ABAP CDS view.

S/4HANA provides DATE_CONVERT_TO_FACTORYDATE standard function module for date-to-factory-date conversion. Base on factory, for example, CA Canada:

 

The function module converts date to closest next factory date (default "+" conversion option):

or closest prior factory date ("-" conversion option):

The same date-to-factory-date conversion can be achieved using custom-built ABAP CDS view 

All it takes a simple custom built ABAP CDS view

Above code does a trick transforming pivoted and nested factory calendar data 

into factory calendar on date level

followed by a couple of window functions to find next and previous factory date for first week-end / holiday day 

and copy it over to other week-end / holiday dates as well fill next factory date / previous factory date for weekdays.

Source code for the CDS view can be downloaded below:

ZI_FactoryCalendar 

ZCL_FACTORY_CALENDAR
